response:
Both probes converge on the same fault line: the checker's false-accept rate is the load-bearing variable, and neither probe found a reason to trust it will clear the bar on the first full-grid attempt. That argues for the staged option ISSUE-109.md itself already sketches, not the full grid and not an outright decline.

## Decision: Proceed, staged — checker-fidelity bolt-on first, full grid gated on clearing the bar

**Chosen option:** Option 2 from the assumption probe. Measure checker false-accept rate cheaply against the existing `benchmarks/2026-07-10-tiered-execution/` haiku-solo/sonnet-solo artifacts before running any new escalate-and-reship cells. Only fund the full grid if a checker variant clears ~10-15% false-accept, as ISSUE-109.md already specifies.

**Why not Option 1 (full grid now):** The premortem's strongest point is that the proposed checker is the same class of model doing the same judgment call that just false-accepted 25% of the time (ADR 0035 assumption log, `docs/decisions/0035-tiered-execution-verdict.md:17`). Committing to the full grid before checking whether *any* variant moves that number is exactly the "extra calls exceed savings" shape that sank tiered at 2.94x (ADR 0035:16) — except here the spend would be wasted before the one number that determines viability is even measured.

**Why not Option 3 (decline outright):** Tiered's rejection was specifically about coach-and-retry converging to the implementer's ceiling (ADR 0035: "iterating a weak implementer against a strong critic converges to the implementer's ceiling"). Routing's mechanism — one Haiku attempt, binary escalate-whole-task, no coaching — is not that mechanism. ADR 0035 itself carries a REOPEN-IF for adjacent configs at different granularity/shape. Declining without measurement rejects an untested hypothesis on a different config's failure, which the assumption probe correctly flags as premature.

**The single load-bearing assumption:** Checker false-accept rate, measured as a bolt-on scoring of already-finished haiku-solo/sonnet-solo artifacts, validly predicts checker behavior inside the live escalate-and-reship pipeline. If false — if checker judgment differs materially when it's gating a real re-ship decision versus scoring a finished artifact post-hoc — the cheap staged test gives a false green light, and the full grid gets funded on a signal that doesn't hold. This is the one premise about the subject matter (not process) that reverses the decision.

**Failure class being accepted:** A false-positive staged result — the bolt-on measurement shows a checker clearing 10-15% false-accept, but the live pipeline's checker (facing real accept/escalate stakes rather than post-hoc scoring) doesn't reproduce that rate, and the full grid launches on bad footing. This is a bounded failure: the staged probe itself costs almost nothing (reused artifacts, no new cells), so the exposure is one full-grid run at worst — not the invisible-in-production shipped-artifact risk the premortem describes for Option 1, and not the foreclosed-hypothesis risk of Option 3.

**Explicitly not adopted from the premortem:** point 3 (decision-rule finalization timing) is a process/pre-registration concern, not a substance-of-the-call issue — it's already handled procedurally by ISSUE-109.md:16 deferring to ADR 0024 before spend, and doesn't change which option is correct here. It should still be finalized before the full grid runs, but it isn't load-bearing for this decision.

**Revisit trigger:** If the staged checker-fidelity measurement shows no variant materially below ~20-25% false-accept, record the null without running the full grid (per ISSUE-109.md:14) — do not re-litigate; that outcome confirms the premortem's point 1 and closes this issue the same way #41 closed.

I'll record this as ADR 0036 in `docs/decisions/` per repo convention — want me to write that file now?

premortem.response:
Read both `ISSUE-109.md` and the related ADR 0035 (the #41 verdict it builds on) for grounding. Here's the premortem — assume the routing experiment shipped and failed six months later:

**1. The checker inherits the exact false-accept mechanism #41 already measured, just moved one step earlier.** ADR 0035's own assumption log records the Sonnet validator false-accepting 6/24 (25%) final artifacts precisely because Haiku's failures are judgment-heavy but *look* structurally fine ("form holds, substance does not," `docs/decisions/0035-tiered-execution-verdict.md:17`). Issue #109 names this as "the known threat" (`ISSUE-109.md:14`) and gates adoption on a checker beating ~10-15% false-accept — but the checker being proposed is the same class of model (Sonnet) making the same kind of judgment call (is this good enough?) that just failed at 25%. Six months out, the checker either gets tuned against the same 8-task gradient it's graded on (overfits, looks fine in-benchmark) or genuinely can't beat ~20% in production, and every false-accept ships a silent Haiku-floor artifact under a routing label that implies Sonnet-equivalent quality — a worse failure mode than tiered's, because tiered's cost overrun was at least visible per-cell, while routing's failures are invisible until someone audits shipped output.

**2. Escalation rate E creeps toward 1 on the harder tail, reproducing #41's cost-overrun shape.** The cost model (`ISSUE-109.md:10`) is only cheap if E stays low; but a checker strict enough to hit the 10-15% false-accept bar necessarily escalates more, especially on the judgment-heavy tasks where Haiku already loses (ADR 0035:17). If E drifts toward 0.6-0.8 on a realistic task mix (vs. whatever the 8-task gradient happens to show), routing cost approaches `0.016 + 0.7×(checker + 0.055)` — i.e., paying near-full Sonnet-solo cost *plus* the wasted Haiku attempt *plus* checker overhead, the same "extra-calls-exceed-savings" failure that sank tiered at 2.94x tokens (ADR 0035:16). The experiment would have measured E on 8 tasks and generalized it to a task population where escalation is systematically higher.

**3. The decision rule gets finalized (or fudged) after the numbers are in, not before.** ISSUE-109.md:16 explicitly calls the decision rule a "sketch... to be finalized per ADR 0024 before spend" — if that finalization slips and someone reads the verdict off numbers already seen, it repeats the exact violation ADR 0035 flagged as a rejected alternative in the *prior* experiment ("moving the bar after unblinding is the exact failure ADR 0024 D3 forbids," ADR 0035:31). A six-month-later failure here isn't a measurement problem at all — it's an adopt decision that can't be trusted because the pre-registration discipline that made #41's null credible wasn't actually followed for #109.

assumption.response:
## Options & load-bearing dependencies

**Option 1 — Proceed with the full routing experiment as scoped (checker variants + grid).**
Load-bearing premise: a checker exists that can beat ~10-15% false-accept (ISSUE-109.md:14, vs. the measured 25% baseline from #41/ADR 0035:27). If X is false — no checker variant gets meaningfully below 25% false-accept — option 1 flips to Option 3: the grid becomes spend chasing a result ADR 0035 already implies (routing inherits the same escalation-fidelity problem the coached-iteration config had, just with the error moved from "iterate to convergence" to "gate to ship").

**Option 2 — Proceed but staged: measure checker fidelity cheaply first (reusing haiku-solo/sonnet-solo cells from `benchmarks/2026-07-10-tiered-execution/`), defer the full grid until a checker clears the bar.**
Load-bearing premise: checker false-accept rate can be validly measured as a bolt-on to the existing cells — i.e., checker judgment doesn't depend on dynamics only present in the full escalate-and-reship pipeline (ISSUE-109.md:10, "only the checker calls and the composition are new"). If X is false — checker accuracy interacts with how escalation is composed (e.g. differs when it's gating a real re-ship vs. scoring existing artifacts post-hoc) — the "cheap" staged test doesn't actually predict full-grid behavior, and Option 2 collapses into Option 1 (you can't shortcut it, must run the whole thing) or Option 3 (not worth the compounded uncertainty).

**Option 3 — Decline; treat #41/ADR 0035's rejection as covering this shape too.**
Load-bearing premise: routing (single Haiku attempt, no coaching, binary escalate-whole-task) is not mechanistically distinct from the already-rejected tiered config for purposes of the prior verdict. If X is false — routing really is the "actually-validated shape" the cascade literature supports, distinct from coach-and-retry convergence-to-floor (ISSUE-109.md:6) — declining without testing rejects an untested hypothesis on the strength of a different config's failure, which flips to Option 1/2 (ADR 0035:26 itself flags "REOPEN-IF" conditions for adjacent configs, and this is arguably one).
